speaker: interviewer
text: So you're with the Dutch girls. Where did they bring you first-- the first day?

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought us-- Sobibor was divided in three camps. Camp one is where we slept. Camp two was where we worked. And the camp three was the gas chamber. And that was a little bit separate-- little bit away. So when somebody ever even-- we never mentioned it. We smelled it. But we never mention it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first day, when the Germans brought us to camp one, and somehow I saw the people from my hometown-- boys. And I was so happy to see them. I say, hi, how are you? And they didn't even react. And I thought, how come? They didn't remember me? So anyway, they went to work. And then we went to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to work, and we were-- had to sort clothes. We had to sort clothes with big, big suitcases on the floor. And we had to sort clothes first quality, second quality, and we had to look if there was gold or valuables, and things like that. And that's what we did the whole day. And also I found pictures from my uncle. My uncle, when the men in Westerbork, went with this transport with his wife and the children to the gas chamber. So-- but I didn't know what it was. I had no idea. I-- I didn't know what I was doing. I just know that was not so bad work, what I had to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that is the first day. And then we had to go on roll call. And then we came out on 5 o'clock, and there was a big, big fire burning. It started already dark. And you smell the hair and the bone, and-- you-- you make-- you don't know what it was. You had no idea. So you walk-- we had to walk to camp one. And we came out of camp one, there were Jewish inmates what had instruments. The-- one had a violin and one had an accordion-- and what-- and what they took with them, and what they were allowed to have in the camp. So they had to start playing the music front of German, like sadistic-- the fire was burning, and-- and everybody was very down.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was going on, where they knows it wasn't transport from 2,000 people-- and they were killed in four hours. In four hours, they could kill 2,000 people. So-- so-- but we didn't know. We Dutch girls didn't know. And that was-- every day, the-- the Germans had something sadistic that we had to do. And that-- the day was that we had to dance. So that day, Chaim-- my husband is now-- that asked me to dance. And that's the way we met. I-- I didn't think he looks terribly handsome. He looks different than the Dutch boys. He has very strange pants on-- leather. I remember they were so wide, and he had a hat on. And he had no hair. And so-- and so-- and then later, I went to our room where we had to sleep-- where we slept. A room, a barrack with pieces of wood, which you see like in Auschwitz-- exactly the same as by us. And there, I met these people from my hometown. And they told me what that fire was all about. And they told me they came with the wife, and one came with a child. And that these-- they were all very a few weeks before. And that the day-- Saturday, that they were gassed, and dead. And I tell you, it took me a long time to comprehends what-- the-- that really, that I was alive, and the fire was burning. And all these people I came with were dead. And you know, I-- I was already a long time in camp. I couldn't understand it. That I was alive, and how could-- you know, when there came transports from Belgium and from France and from Italy and from Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and-- it's-- it's-- you know, like I was not completely normal. Why couldn't I understand it? I was 11. All these people-- what was happened, what is going on. When-- and I think that-- that saved my sanity, that I couldn't understand what was-- it was just unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, when you was always in fear. You was-- you didn't think. You was just thinking about yourself-- and about Chaim later, when I was with Chaim together. And with the small group where we were together. Because you was-- every minute was, you were just-- every minute somebody was shot. When you didn't look right, they shot you. When you-- when-- you didn't have to do anything. The killing was nothing. When-- you know, for pleasure, the-- the Germans kill you. And there was a group of Dutch people came-- and more Dutch people came. And the Dutch people were-- especially when they're intellectuals from universities. And they didn't-- went through everything in life. They went just straight from university, they came. They died in a week time. They laid down and they didn't eat and they didn't drink. The-- and you couldn't live on the food what-- what-- what you got to eat for Sobibor. Because we got in the morning some black coffee with a piece of bread what was sour. And in the evening, you got some soup what was made from straw. And you couldn't eat it. So when-- what we did, we-- when-- we were happy when a transport came, because we were like-- already like animals. Because when there was not a transport, we died. We couldn't eat the food, and there was nothing to eat. So when a transport came, they came with food. So we stole it. I put it in my bra and in my underpants. And-- and when the Germans saw it when you do it, they shot you. But girls, they didn't do so quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So many times-- once, I remember a boy had a sardine-- a can of sardines he opened. And he-- they killed him almost. And they put him over piece of wood. And we had all to come outside, and we had to see what happened to us when we eat anything of that food what came in the packages.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he didn't care. He didn't care really about life. He didn't-- he didn't care because you had to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. Did-- did Chaim teach you his method of survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he watch-- what was his method of survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a very-- very hyper-- I still am-- and tomboy. And he calmed me down. And he told me, slow down. And we separated us. And I remember many things that he say, no, you can't go. And-- and-- Where there was one German Jew what went to the Germans and told all the secrets what happened. And there were 72 Dutchmen, and he went to the Germans and say that these Dutchmen want to run away. And they-- the Germans-- killed all that 72 Dutchmen. I remember one of these Dutch people from my hometown gave me a little piece of butter. Said to me, when I come back, give me the piece of butter back. So-- so this Dutch-- this German, they killed him. They put a bag over him, and they killed him. And I want to go and see it. And said Chaim, no, you stay here. You're not allowed to go. So he got really-- it was-- he saved my life in many ways. Yes. Chaim--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you used to sabotage a little bit when you were sorting the clothes, if you came across a good piece of clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when the German didn't look, I cut it in pieces. Because it was only sabotage what I think what I could do. Chaim did other things. He-- he took the money, and-- and hide it. Because we had a friend what had a-- burned all the papers. And Chaim put the money in-- in the bag, and then this friend of him found it, and-- and put it in the ground-- hide it in the ground. So-- and I cut up all-- when I had a chance. When they would see that I do it, they shoot me too. That was not allowed. And I cut up clothes, yeah. Because I thought-- I know it went to Germany because we-- I was once in the big building where they have all the clothes together. And the trains-- when the Jews came with-- went full back to Germany from all the clothes from the Jews what have gassed. Clothes and gold, and-- and-- what-- what the SS's stole a lot too. When the SS's-- they had-- they were kings. They could do with us what they want. And they had specially tailors and goldsmiths. One goldsmith-- he-- he was in-- in the book. There's a big write-up about him in-- in the book for Sobibor. And he made gold things for the Germans. And there was a boot-- shoemakers. And-- and they all worked for these SS's. They were the slaves for these SS's. So-- the-- that-- that's the work what they did. I don't know how come I just start talking about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Tell me a little bit about the seeds of revolution. When did people start talking about the uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I didn't know much about it, because they didn't trust the Dutch people. And we were not involved at all. And I didn't know anything about it. And I had, at that time, typhus. So I was just healing from typhus. And so I didn't know much about the uprising at all. Only the last day that I know. But one thing what-- when-- when I had typhus, I was one day in bed. And you couldn't be sick in-- in Sobibor. And when you were sick, you right away went to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day I was in bed, and Frenzel what-- picked me out, say to-- came-- went to the men's barrack and say, all the people were sick had to go out and stayed in the middle of that hall. And were standing in the-- in the-- on the pans. And-- and I know some of them very, very well from-- from the Zionist organization what I belonged to. And they-- and then he went to the woman barrack, and he screamed, who's sick? And he-- and I was the only one. And I came out. Said he, go back to work. And I went back to work. So he saved my life. And all the other ones went to the gas chamber. Of-- not to the gas chamber. They were shot. They didn't go to the gas chamber. They were shot. All the-- So somehow I was always a little bit luck with me. Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: That--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the differences of opinions between the Polish Jews and the Dutch Jews. What did they think of one another?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, I don't know much about that. Because I was not involved so much in it. We-- I didn't talk much with the Polish. The Polish didn't trusted us, our Dutch. One, we didn't speak Yiddish, and we didn't speak Polish, and we spoke German. And so they-- the-- not-- some girls, they were very much involved with the Polanders. One, Katy [INAUDIBLE] was a very beautiful girl. And she went with a-- with a Jewish kapo. And the-- and she-- but we didn't think so much about them. We didn't have so much involved. There's a very interesting story. This girl, she was very beautiful. Everybody thought she was very beautiful. And she went with this kapo--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and she was run away. And she was nine months by the partisans in the woods with another girl, Oolah Stern. These two girls were with the partisan. And the last day, from the-- was already peace. And bullets was-- she was laying. She got a wound when she run away, and it was all infected. So she couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was on a wagon on the-- on the road, and a bullet hit her. The last-- it was already freedom. And hit her. And she died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oolah Stern came through the war. She went to Israel. And she went to Israel. And she was also nine months by the partisan. And the partisan didn't want them because they didn't also speak the language. And they run away. And she-- they suffered tremendous. And-- and they couldn't speak Polish. And so they couldn't help the partisans-- what the Jewish partisans-- in the war much. And they had to sleep on-- on-- on the ice and on the snow, when it was the whole winter when we run away-- in October. And so they suffered a lot. And she went to-- she didn't never talk about it. Her story is just a secret. She told a little bit to me when we-- after the war when we lived in Poland together. So she went to Israel and died very young-- on something-- so had a sickness and she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selma, you didn't know about the uprising. But can you tell me about that morning in October?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Chaim told me to put on a lot of clothes. And try to get a lot of clothes, and get boots, and get all different things. And be on 4 o'clock in some kind of a-- a place. By the way, it was a big building, and there were all medicine from-- all the medicine what the people brought, and it was in that building. And knives, and other things were in that big building-- it was a big house. And he said to be there on 4 o'clock. So I have-- I had sweaters on and a vest on and a leather vest on, and-- and-- and boots on. And I was there on 4 o'clock with Chaim together. And Chaim was there. And-- the-- the uprising was already going on. I was a little bit after 4:00. Anyway, the uprising-- when I was there, the uprising was going on. And one of the people-- there was a kapo-- and he killed five or six SS's. And he was very nervous, of course. And he came out. And he-- and I say, take something for your nerves. And I remember he put it in his mouth, and he spit it out. Said he, I don't need it. And then was also-- was it so far that some-- an SSer, what was-- Niemand was his name. No, not Niemand. I forgot his name. Anyway, Chaim will remember his name. And he was in the office. And the men what had to kill him, what-- what was in the uprising involved. Chaim was not much in the uprising involved, because that he went with me. And this man had to go and kill this German. But at last minute, he got afraid . Said he, I'm afraid, I don't want to go. And said Chaim, you have to go to-- There was a messenger boy what told us how many are dead. Said there are 10 dead, and you have to go. And he say, no, I can't go. So Chaim went inside and took a knife. And he went instead of him. And then at once, Chaim was gone. I didn't see that-- it was-- happened so quick. And I-- I didn't know where Chaim was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went out and he-- went to that barrack-- where-- that place where-- where the officer was. And I heard screaming, like they killed a pig. So when I remember with the farmer, I hear it sometimes. I hear it once that they killed a pig. And it was so noisy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say-- and-- and I run back. And that was Chaim killing with the other SSer, what killed already-- with the other kapo, Vershuvski was his name. And he killed already a whole bunch. And they were together killing this German. And then Chaim-- and then we started on a roll call. And then we had to walk passing the barrack where Chaim come-- came out, and he was full with blood. And I had something-- I cleaned his face. And had a wound on his arm. And I had something-- I think a handkerchief or something-- I put around his arm. And then we came to camp one where we slept. And-- so on the side. And then was-- all people starting running, falling, and screaming, and-- and hurrah, you know. And-- and start screaming to-- to each other. And then there came an-- an Ukraine, and they just killed him down. That-- that when we had a lot of Ukraines police around the camp. They were-- that were a police around camp. And then we start screaming. And Chaim took my  hand, and we started running. And then start shooting, and then he-- the-- Frenzel start shooting all-- all over. And in front I saw him standing with something in front of him. And everybody stopped. There was a little house. And Chaim pulls my hand, and we run. And we run through the main entrance, lucky. And we hear mines all over, going off around us. And we run, and we run, and we run, and we run. And that way, we were out of camp. And you know-- also one of the things that the uprising could happened-- there was-- and Wagner was his name. And he was an SSer in camp. And he was such a dangerous man that he knows everything what was going on in camp. He made also the camp very modern, and he made signs. And-- and he was just-- just a Satan. He was the worst. He could smell when something was going on. And he was not there. He was on-- in Germany, to his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a week away. And that's the reason we could make the uprising. When he would have made in camp, we could never have done the uprising. Because he would have known already ahead there's something going on in that camp, what is not so kosher. I don't know how to say it different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good word.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was not-- so he was just-- he-- I saw him once, that he used to hit a man what was in the wrong spot, and he took a shovel and hit his head in two places-- to shut it. And he had a pleasure in sadistic things to do. So he wasn't there, and that was the reason-- I think that was one of the reason that we could-- And also when there came once an-- an transport from Russia. And it met 20-- met a whole bunch of Russian military Jews. They came from Minsk. Minsk was a ghetto. And the Germans were pulling back in that time, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they came. And they give us the courage to run away. And there was one-- [RUSSIAN]-- one Jew. And he wants to run away the first day. I'm terrible in names. I forgot his name. Chaim will remember. And he want to run away the first day. But they told him, you cannot run away. There is a plan, and you have to be very careful. So they-- they calmed him down. So he didn't run away. The-- and there was a plan. Now, because he had a lot of courage, and he was in military, and he knows how to make the plan. And he made the whole plan up, how to run away. And that was his idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, describe your feelings. You went to the forest. You went into the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We came into the forest. And I remember that I had a whole necklace full with all Jewish little things on it. And the first thing what I did is throw it away, because I though, oh, I'm in freedom, you know. And-- so-- and still we're running and running, and slowly I took all my clothes off, what were so hot. And we're running and we're running and we're running, and we running. And we did only three miles when we run around. And we came together in the woods, and we met a whole bunch of people from Sobibor. And they saw me. And Chaim asked if we can go with them. And they say, no. And they had-- one had a gun, a rifle. And they want to shoot Chaim. And I jumped in front of him. And-- and that made him, I think, not-- that they-- that they didn't shoot Chaim. And so they went their way. And Chaim and I went our way, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't want you to come because you were Dutch?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because I'm Dutch. No. And I'm--I don't speak the language, and I'm just an-- a bother. So we came-- we came to-- and then we walked farther. And the first night, we went to a little village. When we were next to Sobibor, we were not far from Sobibor. And we came to a little village. And Chaim had money because the-- the fireman and him divided the money what they had in the ground. So Chaim had some-- I don't know what. He had diamonds in an-- in an-- from the glasses. You put your glasses in its own case, and briefcase. And he had diamonds and gold dollars-- and I don't know what he had. He had it all over him. And so he give this-- asked this man if he will hide us, and he give him a gold piece. And the man say yes. And so we stayed in the attic. And in the same village, the Germans-- and we stayed there the daytime. At nighttime, we walked. And in daytime, we try to find a hiding place. And the first night, the German came to that village and they took a-- lots of Jews from Sobibor--

speaker: interviewer
text: Recaptured them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they all to get-- and somehow they didn't came to the house where we were. So then we started walking. And we walked-- I think 10 days, two weeks. I have no idea. We walked at night. In daytime-- at night. And in daytime, we were hidden. Sometimes we were hidden with the-- but most of the time we were hidden in the woods. Or I remember once we were hidden in a haystack. And-- and little kids came and they start jumping on top of us. And so we-- and they say, Jews, Jews. When they know, of course, from Sobibor, the uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we run out of there. And there came two people with a wagon. And they say, would you like that-- that we take you? And they didn't know that Chaim speak Polish. And when we were driving with these people in the wagon, they say, oh, we can just now-- now we have a good thing. We can take this money from them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to-- Chaim says to me-- scream to me-- Selma, jump. And we jumped off from the wagon, and they went farther. And we run away. So it was-- you know, I-- I remember that I want to go a little glass of water. Because we didn't have to eat. There was no-- we didn't have anything to eat. A little bit, sometimes, we got from the farmer. Once I asked a glass of water. And I saw sitting on very beautiful farm, and the woman didn't even want to give me a glass of water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we walked together, and we hide at night. And it was very scary in the big woods. And I was scared, and he was scared, and I made him scared. And he say to me, you know, you have to stop talking, one. I saw-- in everything I saw the Germans in it. And once we had to go in a big woods, and there was a body laying there in the front.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and it was a very scary thing, and scary time, and-- and very dangerous. Sometimes you hear a dog barking, so-- from a house. Or you-- you-- it was just-- it was-- it was raining, and it was in October. And I remember that I took my boots off, and I couldn't get my boots on anymore. And so we cut it open. And I was walking on the side of these boots all the time. And I-- and my clothes were wet. And it was just horrible. I had slacks on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, finally, after weeks and weeks, Chaim went to a-- we came to a farmer. And Chaim say to him, can we-- can-- can you give us a place to hide? I have money. And-- and the man say, you can stay here overnight in the barn, in the hay. My-- I cannot have you here. I have to-- I have a brother that lived very isolated, and I will bring you there. So-- These were Polish Christians.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish Christians. And so he put us in a wagon, and put Chaim on the bottom, and put branches and wood and things over him. And he dressed me like I'm looking now, like an old lady, with a handkerchief--

speaker: interviewer
text: Babushka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Babushka. And he brought us to this farmer. And this farmer-- we-- I was-- we had-- and the farmer say yes. He will hide us. We were nine months with this farmer. We-- we were on the lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we had-- and skin disease. How I call the skin disease? I forgot. I think-- and skin disease that your skin goes off. See, I miss Chaim. He should sit here and tell me. Eczema?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not eczema. Some kind of a skin disease from dirt. From your dirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- that itched. And it's very, very contagious. And we had it over our whole body. I had it more than Chaim had it. And we had boils from it-- from-- from the lice what we had. And so the first day when we were there, we were allowed to wash ourselves. When we-- when he saw, with all the sickness what we had. But he saw also all the money what Chaim had. And perhaps that helped us, that he saved us lives. And-- so we were nine months hidden there. The same thing to be friend of Chaim what helped him-- it was the fireman, where he divided the money with. He came to a farmer. And the farmer say, yes, you can stay here. And he came at night with the hay fork and killed him. So we were very lucky. We stayed by this farmer nine months. We were together with the cows. The cows were below. And there were pieces of wood, and it was straw over. And there we were not allowed to walk. We're not allowed to talk. We were not-- we couldn't get out anymore because we-- we didn't wash ourselves. We had a pail in the front, and that was our toilet. Especially what kind of a food we got, that smelled sometimes. We didn't-- we didn't-- it was-- it was horrible. But the man saved our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowak. Adam Nowak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Adam Nowak.

speaker: interviewee
text: We send money to these people. See, now we-- we send every few months money, and we help these people. They were very, very poor. They had nothing. Where-- you had no idea how poor they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the woman-- and the weave-- spinning. How do you call it-- on the wheel, she made her own yarn. So I started knitting when I had nothing to do. I started knitting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the neighbors saw what a nice sweaters and socks the kids on had. And how where she got it from when they was so poor, that these kids did have anything. So I'm not allowed to knit anymore for them, because the neighbors thought-- so-- you know. And so also she-- they brought us in the morning a piece of bread. And we put it between our own heads. And we were not allowed to sleep at the same time. Well, Chaim snores. Oh, I snore perhaps too. So when he slept, I was not allowed to sleep, and I slept. And so it-- it was still a very scary time because once, he were-- I remember he had something to drink. And he came to our farm, with a neighbor. And we thought, oh God, now is it the time that he-- you know, he tells this neighbor that we are hidden them. You know, he would be killed. His children will be killed. And the farm would be burned down, and everything. So still, we had the-- we had bread between our head, always saved a little bit of bread. And we woke up sometimes, and-- where-- where we fell asleep together, the rats took the-- you know. So it-- it was-- but still, these people saved our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever tell you a reason? Did you ever talk to them about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well-- well, once-- once we-- we had to go away where we slept. And we-- they put us together with the pigs. And we stayed with the pigs. And then they said-- they said we didn't give them all the money. You know, because-- I don't know how much money. We didn't know what-- how much money it was. No, anyway, and so we stayed with the pigs. But later we could go back to where we slept, which was always better than sleeping together with the pigs. But where-- in the summer, was it so hot, and so many fleas from the cows, that it was just-- we were the whole day just laying nude and cleaning from the-- from the heat. And-- and under the straw-- straw, you know, with little holes, was all another-- there were storks on the chimney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Storks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we didn't have anything to do. So we were looking between this hole at the storks. And once the storks went away. So-- and the reason-- and the-- the Polish think that when the storks go away, there is something very dangerous happening-- a fire or something. So he told us to-- that we had to go away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a superstition.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a superstition. We had to go away. So-- so said he. And then he comes with the-- do you-- do you watch perhaps the storks the whole day? Said we, yes, we have-- we have nothing else to do. So we stopped looking at the storks, and the storks came back. And we didn't have to go away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What a wonderful story.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, I got pregnant, but I didn't know, when I was hidden. And a woman saw me once, and she say, she had to go away. She's pregnant. They have to go away. And one of us, he made his own liquor. And he give Chaim once liquor. I don't drink at all. And Chaim got very drunk because we were not nourished well. We had nothing. We-- I mean-- the-- he brought us food, but it was not-- you know, he brought us in the morning. And there was enough for the whole day. He couldn't do it more often, it was too dangerous.
